general.purpose: staq is a compiler and software toolkit for the openQASM language whose goal is to provide transformation, optimization, and compilation tools on a common language while targeting different simulation and hardware platforms.

input_instruction.evidence: operating on a single textual language, openQASM [15].

output_type.evidence: Finally, staq can generate quantum code in a variety of formats that encompass many of the currently available quantum platforms [15, 18, 19, 9, 10, 12].

automation_level.evidence: staq achieves complex functionality via combining (piping) small tools, each of which performs a single task using the most advanced current state-of-the-art methods.

evaluation_type.evidence: The remainder of this paper describes the staq toolkit and its functionality and uses cases while providing a set of benchmarks (Section 2),

architecture.design_principles: The architecture emphasizes a UNIX-style philosophy of composing many small single-purpose tools into larger workflows. It also follows a Clang-style, syntax-tree-based modular compiler design so transformations can be applied independently and combined flexibly.
architecture.technological_foundation: Implemented in standard C++ using the C++17 standard, with a Clang-inspired approach based on QASM syntax trees and Visitor classes. It includes source-to-source compiler/transpiler tooling targeting formats such as Quil, ProjectQ, Q#, and Cirq.

orchestrator.evidence: The main command line compiler staq - oﬀers a ﬂexible pass registration system, whereby passes are given via command-line arguments and are executed in the order given.

vendor_agnostic_layer.evidence: Finally, staq can generate quantum code in a variety of formats that encompass many of the currently available quantu m platforms [15, 18, 19, 9, 10, 12].

backend_integration.evidence: Along with the default QASM output, staq includes a suite of source-to-source compilers or "transpilers", currently supporting output to Quil [9], ProjectQ [10], Q# [12], and Cir q [18].

user_interface.evidence: The main command line compiler staq - oﬀers a ﬂexible pass registration system, whereby passes are given via command-line arguments and are executed in the order given.

algorithms.overall_evidence: "staq currently implements three layout generation algorithms: linear, eager, and best-fit."

algorithms.name: LHRS (LUT-based Hierarchical Reversible Logic Synthesis)
algorithms.evidence: "synthesized over the Cliﬀord group and T gates using the EPFL implementation of the LUT-based Hierarchical Reversible Logic Synthesis (LHRS) framework [23]."

algorithms.name: Gray-synth
algorithms.evidence: "For these purposes staq includes an optimization pass - CNOT resynthesis to reduce the number of cx (i.e. CNOT) gates in a program by performing the CNOT-dihedral algo rithm Gray-synth of Amy, Azimzadeh, and Mosca [25]."

algorithms.name: linear
algorithms.evidence: "staq currently implements three layout generation algorithms: linear, eager, and best-fit."

algorithms.name: eager
algorithms.evidence: "staq currently implements three layout generation algorithms: linear, eager, and best-fit."

algorithms.name: best-fit
algorithms.evidence: "staq currently implements three layout generation algorithms: linear, eager, and best-fit."

algorithms.name: permutation-based mapping (swap)
algorithms.evidence: "staq implements a version of permutation-based mapping (swap) where for a CNOT gate between uncoupled qubits, the endpoints are swapped along the shortest (weight ed) path in the coupling graph until they are adjacent."

algorithms.name: steiner
algorithms.evidence: "Along with the swap mapping algorithm, staq includes a mapping algorithm ( steiner) based on constrained CNOT and CNOT-dihedral synthesis in the style of [34] a nd [35]."

algorithms.name: constrained Gray-synthesis
algorithms.evidence: "staq performs constrained Gray-synthesis by delaying CNOT gates until a partition of size 1 is reached."

challenges.statement: Some optimization routines may become prohibitively expensive because repeated simplification to a fixpoint can make runtime quadratic in program length.
challenges.category_evidence: In some cases this extra cos t making the optimization quadratic in the length of the program - may b e prohibitive.
challenges.strength_evidence: In some cases this extra cos t making the optimization quadratic in the length of the program - may b e prohibitive.

challenges.statement: Scalability of the optimization algorithms remains an open issue as the number of qubits grows.
challenges.category_evidence: It remains a focus of future work to im prove the scalability of staq's optimization algorithms as the number of qubits increases.
challenges.strength_evidence: The best results in either case are identiﬁed in bold. For circuit optimizatio n, staq beats Qiskit

challenges.statement: Hardware mapping quality is inconsistent, with mixed benchmark results and worse CNOT counts than Qiskit in most cases.
challenges.category_evidence: While the Steiner mapping algorithm with best-fit initial layout was consistently orders of magnitude faster than Qisk it's default mapping algorithm, Qiskit outperformed staq in terms of CNOT counts in the majority of cases.
challenges.strength_evidence: the experimental results for hardware mapping were mixed.

challenges.statement: The current layout optimization may get trapped in local minima, and more sophisticated search methods may be needed.
challenges.category_evidence: It remains to be seen whether more sophisticated layout optimization a lgorithms which avoid local minima for instance, simulated annealing - can improve the results further .
challenges.strength_evidence: It remains to be seen whether more sophisticated layout optimization a lgorithms which avoid local minima for instance, simulated annealing - can improve the results further .

challenges.statement: Support for local ancilla allocation is limited by non-standard QASM and poor compatibility with most QPUs, requiring automatic ancilla management during compilation.
challenges.category_evidence: As local ancilla allocation is non-standard QASM and moreover not sup ported by most QPUs, staq performs automatic ancilla management during the inlining phase of co mpilation.
challenges.strength_evidence: As local ancilla allocation is non-standard QASM and moreover not sup ported by most QPUs
